front: Building: small perfectly circular martyrium in the courtyard of San Pietro in Montorio in Rome
Building: commissioned by Ferdinand and Isabella of Spain to commemorate the site of St. Peter's crucifixion
Building: surrounded by sixteen Doric columns accessible after climbing three steps on its base
Building: uses several orders including Tuscan columns surrounding a Doric-appearing set of pilasters
Building: Bramante's original design for this Vatican basilica used a Greek-cross plan with a dome modeled on the Pantheon, commissioned by Julius II
Building: after Julius II died, Bramante was replaced as architect by Sangallo, Fra Giocondo, and Raphael
Building: Michelangelo later retained and simplified Bramante's Greek cross floor plan after reluctantly accepting this commission
Building: a medallion by the goldsmith Caradosso is one of the few surviving hints at the original external design of this basilica
Architect: earned the Roman nickname 'Il Ruinate' for demolishing so many old buildings to make room for new ones
Building: series of terraced outdoor spaces at the Vatican Palace designed for Julius II, where the Apollo Belvedere and Laocoon and His Sons were originally displayed
Building: contains a section below Pirro Ligorio's nicchione — the largest niche built since antiquity — featuring an ancient bronze pinecone
Architect: served as court architect to Ludovico Sforza in Milan
Building: designed the sixteen-sided dome of Santa Maria delle Grazie in Milan
Building: trompe-l'oeil painting at Santa Maria presso San Satiro makes an alcove appear to be an entire choir around the altar
Architect: namesake double-helix staircase in the Vatican Museums was commissioned by Julius II; a later version was designed by Giuseppe Momo
Architect: designed the Palazzo Caprini, which served as Raphael's residence and influenced Palladio's urban palazzi before being demolished in the 17th century
Architect: namesake cloister is located in Santa Maria della Pace

Architect: according to Condivi, jealously arranged for Michelangelo to paint the Sistine Chapel knowing he had no fresco training, then tried to have him replaced by Raphael when Michelangelo excelled
Architect: organized a contest judged by Raphael and won by Jacopo Sansovino to determine the correct orientation of the Laocoon's broken arm
Architect: Vasari suggests he was the model for the figure of Euclid using a compass on a black slate at the right of Raphael's School of Athens
